pathways.label: Non-conviction arrest expungement
pathways.summary: A person may seek expungement of a felony or misdemeanor arrest that did not result in conviction when one of these applies: 1 The person was not prosecuted, and the time limit to prosecute has expired. 2 The district attorney declined to prosecute any offense arising from the arrest, including because the person completed pretrial diversion. 3 Prosecution was filed, but the case ended by dismissal, sustained motion to quash, or acquittal. 4 The person was judicially determined factually innocent and entitled to wrongful-conviction compensation. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919667")
ruleClauses: A person may seek expungement of a felony or misdemeanor arrest that did not result in conviction when one of these applies:
1 The person was not prosecuted, and the time limit to prosecute has expired.
2 The district attorney declined to prosecute any offense arising from the arrest, including because the person completed pretrial diversion.
3 Prosecution was filed, but the case ended by dismissal, sustained motion to quash, or acquittal.
4 The person was judicially determined factually innocent and entitled to wrongful-conviction compensation. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919667")

Special DWI diversion timing rule: If the arrest was for DWI or a local equivalent and the person entered pretrial diversion, expungement is not available until five years have elapsed from the date of arrest. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919667")

Required facts Wilma must collect: arrest date, arresting agency, original charge at arrest, docket/case number, final disposition, whether pretrial diversion was completed, and whether the offense was DWI-related.

Arrest did not result in conviction A person may seek expungement of a felony or misdemeanor arrest that did not result in conviction when one of these applies: 1 The person was not prosecuted, and the time limit to prosecute has expired.

I can help screen which Louisiana pathway may apply: no-conviction expungement, misdemeanor expungement, felony expungement, interim expungement, marijuana first-offense expungement, redaction, or a fee-waiver route.
waitingRules: Special DWI diversion timing rule: If the arrest was for DWI or a local equivalent and the person entered pretrial diversion, expungement is not available until five years have elapsed from the date of arrest. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919667")

2 More than five years have passed since completion of sentence, deferred adjudication, probation, or parole, and the person has had no felony conviction during that five-year period and has no pending felony charge. A DA certification is required for the five-year route. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919669")

Special marijuana rule: A first-offense misdemeanor conviction for possession of marijuana, THC, or chemical derivatives may be filed 90 days after conviction. Louisiana also has a special Article 998 form for this category. The special fee cap in Article 983(M) is scheduled to terminate on August 1, 2026, so Wilma should not assume that reduced-cost structure after that date without checking the statute. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919669")

2 More than ten years have passed since completion of sentence, deferred adjudication, probation, or parole; the person has no other criminal conviction during the ten-year period before filing; and no criminal charge is pending. A DA certification is required.

Louisianas first-offender pardon statute says a first offender never previously convicted of a felony is automatically pardoned upon completion of sentence without Board of Pardons recommendation or governor action, but no pardon is issued unless court costs for the conviction are paid. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=79202")

Felony hard exclusions / review flags: Article 978 generally blocks expungement for crimes of violence, sex offenses or offenses against minor victims, many controlled-dangerous-substance offenses outside listed exceptions, and domestic abuse battery. Drug convictions may still be eligible if they fall into Article 978s exceptions, including certain possession offenses, possession with intent to distribute, CDS offenses punishable by not more than five years, Article 893(E) cases, or first-offender-pardon cases. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919670")

Special violent-offense exception: After a contradictory hearing, the court may expunge records for aggravated battery, second degree battery, aggravated criminal damage to property, simple robbery, purse snatching, or illegal use of weapons/dangerous instrumentalities if more than ten years have passed since completion, the person has had no criminal conviction during that ten-year period, and has no pending charge. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919670")

when.sourceConditionText: Louisiana expungement means removing arrest or conviction records, photographs, fingerprints, dispositions, and related information from public access. It does not destroy the record. Expunged records remain available to law enforcement, criminal justice agencies, prosecutors, certain licensing/regulatory entities, courts, and other statutorily authorized users. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919663")

In Louisiana, expungement usually means your record is hidden from public view, not erased from existence. Some government, law enforcement, court, licensing, and authorized background-check users may still be able to access it.

If the person is in the physical custody of the Department of Public Safety and Corrections serving a hard-labor sentence, they may not file a motion to expunge an arrest or conviction record at that time. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919666")
Louisiana has a filing bar for people currently in DPS&C custody serving a hard-labor sentence. You may need to wait until that custody status changes or speak with an attorney.

filingDestinationRules: The expungement packet is filed with the clerk of court in the parish of arrest/conviction. Louisiana State Police says forms and required documentation must be filed with that clerk, and Article 972.1 gives district courts, city/parish courts, mayors/traffic courts, juvenile/family courts, and New Orleans municipal/traffic courts authority within their trial jurisdiction. ("https://lsp.org/about/leadershipsections/support/bcii/expungements/")

After filing, the clerk serves the motion by U.S. mail or electronically on the district attorney, Louisiana Bureau of Criminal Identification and Information, and arresting law enforcement agency. If the court grants expungement, the clerk serves the order and judgment on the district attorney, BCII, sheriff, and arresting agency. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919671")

Agencies may object. In general, an objecting agency must file an affidavit of response within 60 days from service. If an objection is timely filed, there is a contradictory hearing, and the objecting agency must show by a preponderance of the evidence why expungement should not be granted. If no objection is filed, the user may waive the hearing and the court must grant the motion if the court determines the mover is legally entitled. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919672")

sourceSections.title: Core Louisiana rule
sourceSections.text:
Louisiana expungement means removing arrest or conviction records, photographs, fingerprints, dispositions, and related information from public access. It does not destroy the record. Expunged records remain available to law enforcement, criminal justice agencies, prosecutors, certain licensing/regulatory entities, courts, and other statutorily authorized users. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919663")
Wilma plain-language explanation:
In Louisiana, expungement usually means your record is hidden from public view, not erased from existence. Some government, law enforcement, court, licensing, and authorized background-check users may still be able to access it.

sourceSections.title: Wilma eligibility decision tree
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Is the person currently incarcerated at hard labor?
If the person is in the physical custody of the Department of Public Safety and Corrections serving a hard-labor sentence, they may not file a motion to expunge an arrest or conviction record at that time. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919666")
Wilma status: hard_stop_current_hard_labor_custody
Wilma script:
Louisiana has a filing bar for people currently in DPS&C custody serving a hard-labor sentence. You may need to wait until that custody status changes or speak with an attorney.

Step 2 - Did the arrest result in a conviction?

sourceSections.title: Arrest did not result in conviction
sourceSections.text:
A person may seek expungement of a felony or misdemeanor arrest that did not result in conviction when one of these applies:
1 The person was not prosecuted, and the time limit to prosecute has expired.
2 The district attorney declined to prosecute any offense arising from the arrest, including because the person completed pretrial diversion.
3 Prosecution was filed, but the case ended by dismissal, sustained motion to quash, or acquittal.
4 The person was judicially determined factually innocent and entitled to wrongful-conviction compensation. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919667")
Special DWI diversion timing rule: If the arrest was for DWI or a local equivalent and the person entered pretrial diversion, expungement is not available until five years have elapsed from the date of arrest. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919667")
Wilma status labels:likely_eligible_no_convictionnot_eligible_yet_dwi_diversion_wait_5_yearsneeds_disposition_proof
Required facts Wilma must collect: arrest date, arresting agency, original charge at arrest, docket/case number, final disposition, whether pretrial diversion was completed, and whether the offense was DWI-related.

sourceSections.title: Misdemeanor conviction
sourceSections.text:
A misdemeanor conviction may be expunged if either:
1 The conviction was set aside and prosecution dismissed under Article 894(B); or
2 More than five years have passed since completion of sentence, deferred adjudication, probation, or parole, and the person has had no felony conviction during that five-year period and has no pending felony charge. A DA certification is required for the five-year route. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919669")
Misdemeanor hard exclusions: Wilma should flag legal review or likely ineligibility if the misdemeanor conviction arose from a sex offense, was for domestic abuse battery, or was for stalking. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919669")
Special marijuana rule: A first-offense misdemeanor conviction for possession of marijuana, THC, or chemical derivatives may be filed 90 days after conviction. Louisiana also has a special Article 998 form for this category. The special fee cap in Article 983(M) is scheduled to terminate on August 1, 2026, so Wilma should not assume that reduced-cost structure after that date without checking the statute. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919669")

sourceSections.title: Felony conviction
sourceSections.text:
A felony conviction may be expunged if the person is not blocked by Article 978(B) and one of these applies:
1 The conviction was set aside and prosecution dismissed under Article 893(E).
2 More than ten years have passed since completion of sentence, deferred adjudication, probation, or parole; the person has no other criminal conviction during the ten-year period before filing; and no criminal charge is pending. A DA certification is required.
3 The person is entitled to a first offender pardon for the offense, as long as the offense is not a crime of violence or sex offense. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919670")
Louisianas first-offender pardon statute says a first offender never previously convicted of a felony is automatically pardoned upon completion of sentence without Board of Pardons recommendation or governor action, but no pardon is issued unless court costs for the conviction are paid. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=79202")
Felony hard exclusions / review flags: Article 978 generally blocks expungement for crimes of violence, sex offenses or offenses against minor victims, many controlled-dangerous-substance offenses outside listed exceptions, and domestic abuse battery. Drug convictions may still be eligible if they fall into Article 978s exceptions, including certain possession offenses, possession with intent to distribute, CDS offenses punishable by not more than five years, Article 893(E) cases, or first-offender-pardon cases. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919670")
Special violent-offense exception: After a contradictory hearing, the court may expunge records for aggravated battery, second degree battery, aggravated criminal damage to property, simple robbery, purse snatching, or illegal use of weapons/dangerous instrumentalities if more than ten years have passed since completion, the person has had no criminal conviction during that ten-year period, and has no pending charge. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919670")
Multiple felonies: A person may have more than one felony conviction expunged in a ten-year period if each felony is independently eligible under Article 978. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919670")

sourceSections.title: Filing and court process
sourceSections.text:
The expungement packet is filed with the clerk of court in the parish of arrest/conviction. Louisiana State Police says forms and required documentation must be filed with that clerk, and Article 972.1 gives district courts, city/parish courts, mayors/traffic courts, juvenile/family courts, and New Orleans municipal/traffic courts authority within their trial jurisdiction. ("https://lsp.org/about/leadershipsections/support/bcii/expungements/")
After filing, the clerk serves the motion by U.S. mail or electronically on the district attorney, Louisiana Bureau of Criminal Identification and Information, and arresting law enforcement agency. If the court grants expungement, the clerk serves the order and judgment on the district attorney, BCII, sheriff, and arresting agency. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919671")
Agencies may object. In general, an objecting agency must file an affidavit of response within 60 days from service. If an objection is timely filed, there is a contradictory hearing, and the objecting agency must show by a preponderance of the evidence why expungement should not be granted. If no objection is filed, the user may waive the hearing and the court must grant the motion if the court determines the mover is legally entitled. ("https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=919672")

sourceSections.title: Packet logic for RCAP/Wilma
sourceSections.text:
If no conviction
Generate/check:
1 Motion for Expungement - Art. 989
2 Order - Art. 991
3 Order of Expungement - Art. 992
4 Affidavit of Response / No Opposition route - Art. 990, if available
5 Fee Waiver Certification - Art. 988, if eligible
6 Supplemental sheets if multiple charges/counts
If misdemeanor conviction
If Article 894(B) was not already completed but appears available:
1 Motion to Set Aside Conviction and Dismiss Prosecution - Art. 987
2 After set-aside/dismissal, Motion for Expungement - Art. 989
3 Order and Order of Expungement - Arts. 991-992
If using five-year route:
1 DA certification of no felony conviction during period and no pending felony bill/indictment
2 Motion for Expungement
3 Order and Order of Expungement
If first-offense misdemeanor marijuana possession:
1 Article 998 marijuana-specific Motion for Expungement
2 Order/Order of Expungement as required by local court
3 Fee analysis: special $300 cap applies only while Article 983(M) remains in effect
If felony conviction
If Article 893(E) set-aside route:
1 Motion to Set Aside Conviction and Dismiss Prosecution - Art. 987, if not already done
2 Motion for Expungement - Art. 989
3 DA certification if required by the selected basis
4 Order and Order of Expungement
If ten-year route:
1 Confirm ten years since completion
2 Confirm no criminal conviction in ten-year window
3 Confirm no pending charge
4 Obtain DA certification
5 File Motion for Expungement and proposed orders
If first-offender-pardon route:
1 Confirm first felony status
2 Confirm sentence complete
3 Confirm court costs paid
4 Confirm offense is not excluded
5 Attach first-offender pardon proof/certificate if available
